story:
The morning news announces New Jersey’s black bear hunt. Randall Beck clicks off the TV, checks his permit, and steps into the cold, crisp air, ready to hunt. His neighbor, Margaret “Maggie” Cassidy, catches him on the walkway. Eyeing his rifle, she scolds, “Everything has a right to live.” Randall, impatient, replies that it’s a legal management hunt and a public service. Her disapproval only deepens. His sputtering truck finally turns over. As he pulls away, Maggie’s eyes glint. “I expect you will, Mr. Beck.”

He parks in a secluded spot, full of energy and visions of a trophy. In the woods he finds a fresh trail: tracks, scat, a rubbed tree. Intertwined with the pawprints are human footsteps. Someone else is ahead. He edges into a clearing and sees a huge blue-black bear—massive for a black bear—sitting calmly with its back to him. Randall raises his rifle. A rustle to his left; a second bear, even bigger, launches from the brush. He throws up the rifle to block a swiping paw; the gun is ripped away. With two bears roaring behind him, Randall bolts, branches clawing his face. He drops his pack, hoping the food will distract them. It doesn’t.

He reaches his truck, fights the stubborn door, and tumbles inside. The key does nothing. Glass explodes as a bear’s paw cleaves the passenger window. The truck rocks under their weight. The engine coughs to life; Randall guns it, fishtails, overcorrects, and smashes into an oak. Dazed and bleeding, he staggers onto the road. A lone car slows. He climbs in. “Thank you.”

The driver studies him. He recognizes her: “You’re that reporter—Angelina Uri…”
“Urisdae,” she says. “Right, Maggie Cassidy’s daughter—the old family name your folks used before they changed it when they came to America,” Randall says. She glances at his blood and torn camo. “Out hunting?”

Not in the mood, Randall snaps that he doesn’t want any “bleeding-heart liberal” lecture. She says nothing, turns down a rutted lane, and stops at a weathered lodge. On the steps, she asks, carefully, “Do you think it’s okay to hunt living creatures just for the sake of hunting?”

He hesitates. A rustle answers for him. Margaret Cassidy steps from the trees with her husband, Herb. “Hi, Mom. Hi, Dad,” Angelina says.

He frowns. “What’s going on?”

Angelina explains that “Urisdae” is their old family name. Her parents changed it when they came to America.
“Joining the rest of the bear hunters,” Angelina says. People file out of the lodge, hard-eyed, silent. Randall scoffs—Maggie hates hunting. Angelina’s smile turns sharp. “You should have taken her advice.”

“When I say bear hunters,” she adds, “I mean bears doing the hunting.”

Randall swallows. “Hunting whom?”

“You.” She tips her head. “A little Latin: black bears are family Ursidae—almost our name.”

Then the air fills with tearing cloth and guttural cries. Angelina’s face ripples; her nose and jaw lengthen; dense black fur bursts along her arms. Maggie and Herb drop to all fours as their bodies contort, howls rolling out of them. The others follow, shoulders hunching, torsos swelling with muscle and hair. Where people stood, black bears now huff and stare.

Randall turns and runs, the woods ringing with thunderous pursuit.
